# Preface {.unnumbered}

This is the course reader for *Bio196-22 Computing for Bioinformatics*. It is a focused
six-week companion to a broader computing-skills reference, organized around the order
in which students will need each tool during the course.

The course focuses on the practical computing environment of modern bioinformatics:
working at the Unix command line, editing text files, connecting to remote systems,
running work on high-performance computing clusters, managing software environments,
using containers, understanding sequencing workflows, running reproducible pipelines,
and communicating analyses clearly.

## Who This Book Is For

This book is intended for:

- Graduate students and researchers entering computational biology
- Wet-lab biologists who want to analyze their own sequencing data
- Programmers from other fields looking to apply their skills to the life sciences
- HPC administrators and core-facility staff supporting bioinformatics users

## What You Will Learn

By the end of this course reader you will be comfortable with:

- Navigating the Unix command line, manipulating files, and editing plain-text project files
- Connecting to remote systems with SSH and submitting jobs to a Slurm cluster
- Managing software with Conda and packaging tools with Docker and Apptainer/Singularity
- Understanding next-generation sequencing data and the structure of RNA-seq analysis
- Running reproducible workflows with Nextflow and community nf-core pipelines
- Tracking work with Git and GitHub and producing reproducible reports with Quarto

## Book Roadmap

The chapters are organized by week.

**Week 1: Linux Basics and Text Editing** builds the day-to-day foundation: the shell,
file manipulation, text processing, and editing project files.

**Week 2: SSH and High-Performance Computing** moves the work onto remote systems and
introduces the scheduler-centered model of cluster computing.

**Week 3: Conda and Containers** covers practical software management, from Conda
environments to Docker and Apptainer/Singularity containers.

**Week 4: NGS and RNA-seq** connects the computing tools to sequencing data and a
standard RNA-seq analysis workflow.

**Week 5: Nextflow and nf-core** scales single analyses into reproducible workflows and
community-maintained pipelines, including nf-core/rnaseq.

**Week 6: Version Control and Reproducible Reports** closes the course with Git, GitHub,
and Quarto reports so analyses can be inspected, repeated, and shared.

## How to Use This Book

Each chapter includes explanations, worked examples, and exercises. Work through the
chapters in order during the course, then return to specific chapters later as a
reference when setting up projects or troubleshooting analyses.

## Prerequisites

No prior programming experience is assumed, though familiarity with basic biology
concepts (genes, sequences, expression data) will be helpful when reading examples.

---

*Happy computing!*

— Yucheng Zhang and Shirley Li, Tufts University
